statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph with V vertices and E edges, detect if the graph contains a cycle. The vertices are 0-indexed.
statement.inputFormat:
The first line contains two integers V and E, representing the number of vertices and edges in the graph respectively.
Each of the next E lines contains two integers u and v, representing an undirected edge between vertices u and v.
statement.outputFormat: Print 'true' if the graph contains a cycle, and 'false' otherwise.

examples.explanation: The graph is a simple tree and does not contain a cycle.

hints.text: To detect a cycle in an undirected graph, consider the properties of undirected graphs and how they differ from directed graphs in terms of cycles.

Recall that a graph is considered to have a cycle if there is a path that starts and ends at the same vertex, and passes through at least one edge more than once. In an undirected graph, if you encounter a vertex that is already in your current path, it could indicate a cycle, but you need to handle the case where the vertex is the parent of the current vertex.

BFS can be used for cycle detection in undirected graphs by keeping track of visited vertices and their parents. If during traversal you encounter a vertex that has already been visited and it's not the parent of the current vertex, then a cycle is detected.

The key to solving this problem with BFS is to use a data structure to keep track of visited vertices and another to keep track of the parent of each vertex. This allows you to differentiate between a vertex that is part of a cycle and one that is not.

editorial.title: Optimizing Cycle Detection in Undirected Graphs
editorial.intuition: The key observation is that in an undirected graph, a cycle can be detected by keeping track of the parent of each node during the traversal. If we encounter a visited node that is not the parent of the current node, then a cycle exists.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking all possible paths in the graph, which would result in an exponential time complexity. This is impractical for large graphs.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a breadth-first search (BFS) traversal with a parent tracking mechanism allows us to detect cycles in an undirected graph efficiently.
editorial.optimalSolution: The provided solution uses a BFS traversal with a parent tracking mechanism, achieving a time complexity of O(V + E) and a space complexity of O(V + E).
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by observing that if a cycle exists in the graph, the BFS traversal will eventually encounter a visited node that is not the parent of the current node. Conversely, if no such node is encountered, then no cycle exists.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution on a sample graph can help verify its correctness and illustrate its efficiency.
commonMistakes: Failing to keep track of visited nodes, leading to infinite loops.
Not checking for cycles when encountering a visited node.
Using a traversal algorithm that is not suitable for undirected graphs.
Not handling the case where the graph is disconnected.
Incorrectly implementing the parent tracking mechanism.
Not considering the time and space complexity of the solution.

functionSignature.java: public boolean isCyclic(int[][] edges, int V);
functionSignature.python: def isCyclic(self, edges: list[list[int]], V: int) -> bool:
